one_line_takeaway: A position/architecture paper proposing 'Human-Certified Module Repositories' -- a synthesis of SLSA provenance, Sigstore-style signing, human security review, and Azure-Verified-Modules-style curation -- as a conceptual defense against AI-assisted supply-chain compromise, with no implementation or empirical evaluation of its own.
key_finding: There is no empirical finding to report -- the paper's central claim is architectural/normative: that combining human certification, SLSA-style provenance, contract-based interfaces, and multi-tier assurance (a synthesis of existing frameworks: SLSA, Sigstore, Azure Verified Modules, seL4/CompCert-style verification) into a single curated repository model would plausibly have prevented or mitigated the SolarWinds, Log4Shell, and XZ Utils incidents, and would constrain AI coding agents to assemble only vetted modules. This claim is argued qualitatively via the three case-study walkthroughs, not tested.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Human-Certified Module Repositories (HCMRs), a conceptual/architectural model for curated software module repositories intended to support safe AI-assisted software composition. This is a position/vision paper (accepted at IEEE AQTR 2026) with no implementation or empirical evaluation. Its own contribution consists of: (1) a definition and motivation for HCMRs at the intersection of AI-assisted development and supply-chain risk; (2) a reference architecture with six design principles (strong provenance, human certification, composable interfaces with explicit contracts, secure-by-default assembly, multi-tier assurance levels inspired by SLSA/AVM, and ecosystem-scale maintainability against maintainer bottlenecks); (3) a four-stage certification pipeline (intake/automated vetting, human security review, sandboxed behavioral validation, certification/release); (4) a metadata model (provenance attestation, interface contract, security attributes, assurance level, dependency-graph digest); (5) an 'AI-assisted assembly pipeline' concept (constraint-based module discovery, contract-based synthesis, provenance-aware build orchestration, runtime monitoring/continuous attestation); (6) a threat model enumerating five adversary-capability categories and five threat surfaces, mapped in a table to proposed HCMR mitigation mechanisms; and (7) three narrative case-study walkthroughs (SolarWinds, Log4Shell, XZ Utils) explaining qualitatively how the proposed HCMR architecture would have mitigated each incident. No prototype, formal model, dataset, or quantitative evaluation of HCMRs is presented anywhere in the paper.
threat_model: The paper's own Section VI (Threat Model and Mitigation Strategies) defines adversary capabilities drawn from real incidents cited in Related Work: (1) compromise of build infrastructure (SolarWinds-style CI/CD injection into signed update channels), (2) abuse of maintainership/contributor trust via long-term social engineering (XZ Utils-style), (3) exploitation of widely deployed vulnerable components with persistent long-tail exploitation (Log4Shell-style), (4) manipulation of provenance/signing signals (trusted-signature abuse), and (5) abuse of loosely governed modular composition ecosystems (IFTTT/Node-RED-style). It then defines five threat surfaces -- dependency injection, build-system subversion, maintainer account hijacking, key/credential abuse, and insecure automation -- each mapped in Table II to a corresponding proposed HCMR mitigation (module certification+provenance, verified provenance, multi-party review, ephemeral signing, contract-aware composition, respectively). This threat model is a synthesis/categorization of publicly documented incidents rather than a novel formal adversary model with capability bounds, and no attack is actually executed or simulated against an HCMR implementation -- the mitigations are asserted design intentions, not demonstrated defenses.
limitations: The paper itself acknowledges (Section IX, Open Research Challenges) that scalable human certification of thousands of modules, formal/machine-checkable interface contracts, automated detection of maintainer compromise, provenance-enforced build reproducibility at scale, AI-facing guardrail design, and sustainable governance are all unresolved open problems -- i.e., the core mechanisms of HCMRs are not yet worked out even at a design level, only motivated. No prototype exists, no performance/scalability/cost analysis of human certification is given, no comparison against existing package-repository security tooling (e.g., npm/PyPI provenance features, Sigstore adoption in real ecosystems) is empirically made, and the three case studies are retrospective, unfalsifiable illustrations (the architecture is checked against incidents it was explicitly designed around) rather than out-of-sample validation. All quantitative figures cited in the paper (e.g., 18,000 SolarWinds-affected organizations, ~30,000 XZ-dependent Debian/Ubuntu packages, the SUSVIBES benchmark's 61% functional-correctness / 10.5% functional-and-secure results) are figures from cited third-party studies, not results produced by this paper.
